title: Review §12 GET /sse 409 conflict: verify against hello-world logs and add mitigations
description:
The eldamo-server production agent added §12 (409 Conflict on GET /sse) to LESSONS_LEARNED.md based on observations from the eldamo production server. This section needs review against the hello-world server's own log data and, if reproducible, concrete mitigation steps validated against the minimal example.

CONTEXT:
In eldamo-server's production session, GET /sse returned 409 persistently after a Cloud Run deployment rollout. Tool calls (POST /sse → 200) continued working because the MCP SDK has a synchronous response path that bypasses the GET stream. But async (POST → 202) tool results could not be delivered, causing Spark to show 'Thinking it through...' indefinitely with no resolution.

The 409 arises because the MCP Go SDK enforces one active GET stream per session ID. After a rollout, Spark tries to reopen a GET stream on the new instance; the new instance either doesn't know the session (404) or has a conflicting entry (409).

WHAT TO DO:
1. Check the hello-world server's Cloud Run logs for any GET / → 409 responses during or after deployment. Does the simpler server see this pattern? (It may not, given shorter sessions and the /{$} root mount vs /sse named path.)
2. Attempt to reproduce: deploy a new revision mid-session and observe whether 409s appear on the GET stream.
3. Test the '--no-traffic' mitigation: deploy with --no-traffic, then cut over manually. Does this prevent 409s?
4. Check mcp.StreamableHTTPHandlerOptions in the Go SDK for any keepalive/heartbeat option that would prevent the server from treating the GET stream as idle.
5. Update §12 with hello-world-specific observations (does it affect /{$} the same way it affects /sse?), and sharpen or add mitigations based on what you find.
6. Add a checklist item to the Summary checklist if a concrete mitigation is validated.

The section as written is accurate for a named-path (/sse) endpoint on a production server with long-running sessions. The hello-world perspective (root endpoint, short echo sessions) may surface a different profile — or confirm the same pattern.

Refs: eldamo-server epic eldamo-server-n3l; LESSONS_LEARNED.md §12
notes:
UPDATE from eldamo-server agent (2026-07-02): Two mitigations are now validated and implemented in production (eldamo-server commit 96c9b58).

1. SERVER-SIDE KEEPALIVE (primary fix, confirmed working):
   mcp.ServerOptions{KeepAlive: 30 * time.Second} passed to mcp.NewServer.
   The Go SDK sends a ping to the client every 30s on the open GET /sse stream,
   preventing the idle-closure → reopen → 409 cycle. This is the go-to mitigation.
   The 'StreamableHTTPHandlerOptions' referenced in the original task description
   is a red herring — the correct field is ServerOptions.KeepAlive (server.go).

2. --no-traffic DEPLOY FLAG (prevents rollout-induced 409s):
   ./scripts/deploy.sh --no-traffic deploys without shifting traffic.
   Cut over with: gcloud run services update-traffic <name> --to-latest --region <region>
   The deploy script in spark-agent-tools should get this same flag.

LESSONS_LEARNED.md §12 Mitigations section updated with exact code snippet and
concrete gcloud commands. Checklist also updated with both items.

FOR THIS TASK: please verify whether the hello-world server (which uses POST /
root endpoint with shorter sessions) encounters the 409 pattern at all. The
hypothesis is that shorter sessions and the /{$} root mount may behave differently
from the /sse named-path used in eldamo. Add KeepAlive to the hello-world server
regardless — it's a one-liner with no downside.
close_reason: Confirmed: hello-world sees GET / → 409 at 15:33:38 and 15:40:31 (after cold starts/redeploys). Root /{$} vs /sse path makes no difference — SDK enforces one-stream-per-session regardless. Added KeepAlive: 30*time.Second to ServerOptions in hello-world/mcp.go. Updated LESSONS_LEARNED §12 with hello-world-specific observation. --no-traffic validated by eldamo-server agent; checklist items already present.

title: Refactor: move hello-world tool into hello-world/ subdirectory
description: Restructure the repo so each MCP server lives in its own subdirectory, making this a multi-tool monorepo. Move all current root-level Go source (main.go, mcp.go, oauth.go, oauth_test.go) and infra (Dockerfile, scripts/, Makefile) into hello-world/. Update the module path in go.mod to github.com/ghchinoy/spark-agent-tools/hello-world. Update docs/ references accordingly. Add a top-level README that explains the repo layout and links to each tool's subdirectory. Leave the shared auth package (task m28.6) outside the per-tool dirs so all tools can import it. ACCEPTANCE: 'cd hello-world && make test && make run-dev' works; top-level README explains the monorepo layout; CI workflow updated to build/test from the hello-world/ dir.
close_reason: Moved main.go, mcp.go, Dockerfile, scripts/, Makefile into hello-world/. Module path stays at repo root (go.mod shared with pkg/mcpauth). hello-world/Makefile delegates go commands to repo root. CI unchanged — runs from root, covers full module. All quality gates pass.

title: Abstract OAuth 2.1 scaffolding into a shared auth package
description: Extract the reusable OAuth 2.1 server into a standalone Go package (e.g. pkg/mcpauth or internal/mcpauth) so each tool subdirectory (hello-world/, and future tools) can import it with a few lines instead of copying oauth.go. The shared package should expose: NewAuthServer(opts), the four RFC handlers (PRM, AS metadata, DCR, authorize+token), requireBearer middleware, and pluggable interfaces for the client/code stores (in-memory default; implementers can swap in a DB). Per-tool code then becomes just: the tool handlers in mcp.go, route wiring in main.go that calls mcpauth.Mount(mux, authServer), and whatever application-specific logic the tool needs. DESIGN NOTES: keep the pluggable-store interface minimal (RegisterClient, GetClient, SaveCode, ConsumeCode) to avoid over-engineering. The store interface also documents exactly what production needs to persist. Flag the 'no real user login' demo limitation via an AuthorizeHandler hook the caller can override with a real IdP. ACCEPTANCE: hello-world imports the shared package; its main.go and mcp.go contain only tool-specific code; oauth.go in hello-world/ is deleted or reduced to a thin config shim. golangci-lint 0 issues.
close_reason: Extracted oauth.go into pkg/mcpauth with Store interface, NewAuthServer, Mount, RequireBearer, and ResolveSubject hook. Tests moved to pkg/mcpauth. All quality gates pass.

title: Initial seed: echo MCP server + OAuth 2.1 scaffolding + docs
description: DONE in seed commit. Implements: main.go (routing/mux, base-path mount), mcp.go (echo tool + SSE/Streamable multiplexer), oauth.go (RFC 9728 PRM, RFC 8414 AS metadata, RFC 7591 DCR, RFC 7636 PKCE authorize+token, HMAC JWT bearer middleware, in-memory stores), oauth_test.go (full-flow tests). Infra: Dockerfile (distroless), scripts/deploy.sh (Cloud Run source deploy + minimal SA), Makefile, .env.example. Docs: README, TUTORIAL.md, connecting-spark.md, oauth-deep-dive.md, blog-post.md, architecture.dot+webp. go test/vet/fmt all clean; live smoke-tested locally (PRM/AS/DCR/401-challenge/base-probe/404 all correct).
close_reason: Seed complete — echo MCP server, full OAuth 2.1 scaffolding (RFC 9728/8414/7591/7636), tests, Dockerfile, deploy script, and complete docs. Verified: go test/vet/fmt clean, live smoke test of all discovery/auth/probe endpoints.

title: [EPIC] spark-agent-tools: hello-world MCP server + tutorial for Gemini Spark
description: Seed repo demonstrating how to build a secure, Cloud Run-hosted MCP server usable by Gemini Spark as a custom Connected App. Distilled from the eldamo-server OAuth work (RFC 9728 PRM, RFC 8414 AS metadata, RFC 7591 DCR, RFC 7636 PKCE). Self-contained single Go binary, no DB, echo tool. Includes tutorial docs, Spark connection guide, OAuth deep-dive, blog post draft, and architecture diagram.

title: Add a second, more useful sample tool beyond echo
description: Demonstrate a tool that does real work (e.g. fetch/summarize a URL, a simple calculator, or a Google API call) to show how to extend the tool surface in mcp.go without touching the OAuth scaffolding. Keep the echo tool as the minimal reference.

title: Optional: persistence + real user auth variant (production path)
description: Show the production upgrade path referenced in docs/oauth-deep-dive.md: swap in-memory client/code stores for a DB (e.g. Firestore) and add real end-user authentication at the consent step (Google Sign-In / Firebase). Could be a separate branch or a /production subdirectory so the hello-world stays minimal. Cross-reference eldamo-server.

title: Add end-to-end test with a real Gemini Spark connection
description: Deploy to Cloud Run, connect from Spark UI, capture the log trace (HEAD probe -> PRM -> AS -> register -> consent -> token -> tool calls), and add it to docs/connecting-spark.md as a verified real-world trace. Confirms parity with the eldamo-server production result.
close_reason: Completed via live Gemini Spark connections (2026-07-02). Deployed hello-world-spark to Cloud Run, connected from Spark UI, captured full traces with MCP method logging: HEAD probe → PRM → AS → DCR → consent → PKCE token → initialize → tools/list → tools/call. Documented in LESSONS_LEARNED §2 (exact request sequence) and §13-16 (method logging, spec-field support, creative tool use). Confirms parity with eldamo-server production result.
